category_title: Students who prioritize task completion with accuracy but have low efficacy and rely on help-seeking
minimal_context: The student is working on a grade-level reading comprehension assignment during an individualized session with a support teacher.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Please read this grade-level passage and answer the comprehension questions at the end.

STUDENT: I want to advance in reading, so I want to make sure my answers are accurate. But as I read this first part, I asked myself, do I understand what the information is saying? I don't think I do. Can you help me?

TUTOR: Just do what you can.

STUDENT: It's difficult to control my attention in the classroom. If I just do what I can, I'm just going to write down anything to get the task completed instead of trying to understand it.

TUTOR: Keep trying to read the next paragraph.

STUDENT: I need you to help me again. I find everything... confusing. Can you just tell me what the right answers are so I can be done?

options.A: Instruct the student to write the title of the reading passage and record as much as they can recall from beginning to end, reminding them it is a retelling, not a summary.
options.B: Conduct careful pre-intervention functional assessments during the reading sessions to identify relationships between the instructional environment and the student's behavior.
options.C: Emphasize mastery goals during the reading sessions by valuing personal improvement and mastery of the comprehension task rather than grades or social comparison.
options.D: Provide a timer that sounds every minute, having the student self-record whether they displayed appropriate behaviors during the preceding interval by checking Yes or No on a tracking sheet.
